## Actions vs. workflows

:::{.callout-note}
## Composite action

A **composite action** is a list of steps.
It runs *inside* a job that already exists -
the caller's runner and permissions are already in place.
An action has no concept of jobs or triggers;
it is just a named bundle of commands.

```yaml
# <name>/action.yml
name: My Action
description: Does a thing
inputs:
  my-input:
    description: ...
runs:
  using: composite
  steps:
    - run: echo "hello"
      shell: bash
```
:::

:::{.callout-note}
## Reusable workflow

A **reusable workflow** is a complete pipeline.
It defines its own jobs, each with its own runner (`runs-on:`),
permissions (`permissions:`), and steps.
A consumer calls it as a job, not as a step.

```yaml
# .github/workflows/<name>.yml
on: workflow_call
jobs:
  my-job:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    permissions:
      contents: read
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - run: echo "hello"
```
:::

:::{.callout-warning}
## `shell:` is required in composite actions

Every `run:` step in a composite action must declare `shell:`.
Workflows have a default shell; composite actions do not.
:::

|  | Composite action | Reusable workflow |
|---|---|---|
| Key top-level keys | `name`, `description`, `inputs`, `outputs`, `runs` | `on`, `jobs` |
| Jobs | No - only `steps` under `runs` | Yes - one or more `jobs` blocks |
| `runs-on` | Absent - inherits the caller's runner | Per job |
| `permissions` | Absent | Yes (caller must also grant them) |
| Called from | A job's `steps` (via `uses`) | A workflow's `jobs` (via `uses`) |

Because an action cannot declare permissions or choose its runner,
this repo uses the two-layer design:
the composite action holds the real logic,
and the reusable workflow wraps it to handle the runner, permissions, and checkout.
Consumer repos reference only the workflow layer.
